title: Soy wax jar candle with lavender
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle in soy wax, 6% lavender fragrance load
excerpt: A soy wax container candle in a 200 ml glass jar with a pre-tabbed cotton wick and 6% lavender essential oil by wax weight. The burn pool reaches the jar edge in 3-4 hours on the first burn; the candle burns approximately 30-35 hours to the end of the fragrance load.
sourceNotes: Bramble Berry candle-making guides (brambleberry.com, accessed May 2026), soy wax fragrance-load recommendations and pour-temperature guidance. CandleScience soy wax notes (candlescience.com, accessed May 2026), wick sizing by jar diameter.

content.text: A soy wax container candle. Soy wax is a natural vegetable wax with a lower melting point than paraffin (around 50°C versus 55-60°C) and a clean, slow burn. It holds fragrance well at loads up to 10% of the wax weight, though 6-8% is the practical range for clear wax adhesion and consistent throw. This recipe is 6% lavender essential oil, which gives a clean floral scent without the heavy sweetness of a fragrance-oil lavender.

Melt wax on a simmering-water bath, not directly on the hob. Wax above its flashpoint (soy wax around 300°C, though the practical concern is wax above 90°C left unattended) can ignite; water makes a wax fire worse. Keep a kitchen fire blanket within reach. Never leave melting wax unattended.

items.name: Soy wax (container blend)
items.prepNote: container-grade soy wax (lower melting point than pillar-grade, adheres to glass as it contracts). A 200 ml jar takes approximately 140-160 g at full fill.

items.name: Lavender essential oil
items.prepNote: Lavandula angustifolia or lavandin. 6% of the wax weight (9 g, approximately 9 ml). Add when the wax has cooled to 55°C.

items.name: Candle wick, pre-tabbed (CD-12)
items.prepNote: pre-tabbed cotton wick sized for a 7 cm diameter jar (a CD-12 or ECO-8 is a common equivalent). Centred and secured with the wick bar before pouring.

content.text: Press the wick tab firmly to the bottom centre of the empty jar. A small dab of melted wax or a wick sticker holds the tab down. Thread the wick through the wick-centring bar and lay the bar across the top of the jar so the wick is held vertical and centred. The wick should extend 1-2 cm above the top of the bar.

Place the soy wax in the candle pouring pitcher. Set the pitcher in a saucepan of water (not touching the saucepan base; raise it on a trivet if needed). Heat the water to a gentle simmer and stir the wax as it melts. Check the temperature with the probe; remove the pitcher from the water bath when the wax reaches 70°C and stir to even the temperature.

Set the pitcher aside and monitor the temperature as the wax cools. When the probe reads 55°C, add the lavender essential oil and stir for 2 minutes to fully incorporate it into the wax. Soy wax binds fragrance best between 55-65°C; adding at the higher end risks the fragrance burning off, adding below 50°C risks the fragrance not binding and pooling at the base.

When the wax has cooled to 60-65°C, pour steadily into the jar beside the wick, not onto it. A slow, close pour minimises bubbles. Fill to within 1 cm of the top. Do not move the jar for 24 hours; soy wax sets slowly and moving it causes surface cracking. Leave the wick-centring bar in place until the wax is fully firm.

Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn and before every subsequent burn. Light the candle and allow it to burn for at least 3-4 hours, until the melt pool reaches the full diameter of the jar. This is the
: a candle stopped before the melt pool reaches the edge will tunnel on every subsequent burn. Never burn for more than 4 hours at a stretch.

Effectively indefinite unlit in a cool, shaded location away from direct sunlight (UV fades the colour and breaks down the fragrance faster). Lavender essential oil fragrance fades after approximately 2 years in an unlit candle. Burn within 2 years for best scent throw. Discard when the wax is within 1 cm of the bottom of the jar; burning an empty jar overheats the glass and the tab.

attrs.intro: The three ways a soy jar candle goes wrong.

items.fix: Pour at 60-65°C rather than higher. For an existing candle, warm a small amount of wax to the same temperature and top up the sinkhole with a thin pour.
items.cause: The wax contracted as it cooled. Common in soy wax; more pronounced when poured hot.
items.symptom: Sinkholes or craters in the surface as it sets

items.fix: Nothing needed. Warming the jar slightly with a heat gun before the final set can minimise the appearance, but it always returns.
items.cause: Polymorphic crystallisation in soy wax; a natural property, not a defect. Does not affect burn quality.
items.symptom: White frosting on the set surface

items.fix: On subsequent burns, allow longer burns. To rescue a tunnelled candle, wrap the jar in foil and burn for 4+ hours to force the outer wax to melt.
items.cause: The first burn was stopped before the melt pool reached the jar edge.
items.symptom: Candle tunnels down the centre, wax left on the sides

attrs.heading: If the candle went sideways
